weaknesses: Abstract/intro overreach on the range and consistency claims: the abstract states the rate holds 'for d up to a constant multiple of n log n' and that 'consistency ... is equivalent to d=o(n log n)', but Theorem~\ref{obj:thm:sharp-minimax-fixed-interior} asserts the sandwich and the consistency equivalence only inside d\le\rho_\epsilon n\log n, where \rho_\epsilon is an unspecified (possibly tiny) constant. Fix: in the abstract and the third intro paragraph, explicitly attach the 'within d\le\rho_\epsilon n\log n' qualifier to both the rate and the consistency-threshold statements, and say a word about how large \rho_\epsilon may be taken.

The binary-outcome/binary-treatment restriction (Y,A\in\{0,1\}) is never discussed as a limitation, yet it is doing real work (S(O)^2=1, factorial-moment unbiasedness, \tau\in[-1,1] truncation). A referee cannot tell whether the log-gap phenomenon is special to two-cell outcomes. Fix: add a sentence in Section 4 stating whether/how the result extends to bounded or multi-valued outcomes, and flag it as an open direction if it does not.

The lower bound (the source of sharpness, including the log factor) is entirely imported from \citet{ZengBalakrishnanHanKennedy2024} via Lemma~\ref{obj:lem:ate-lower-bound-transfer}, but the intro and Section 4 present the contribution without cleanly separating what is genuinely new (the ATE hybrid upper bound and the one-arm-to-ATE transfer) from what is borrowed (the construction and the log-gap minimax lower bound). Fix: add one or two sentences delineating the novelty boundary explicitly, so the transfer is not read as a new lower bound.

The paper gives no applied or concrete setting in which a covariate has an unrestricted discrete alphabet whose size grows toward n\log n; the motivation stays entirely abstract, which weakens the case that the d^2/(n^2\log^2 n) term is practically relevant. Fix: add a short motivating example in the Introduction (e.g. many-category fixed effects, fine ZIP-code/product strata) that plausibly realizes the growing-alphabet regime.

Definition~\ref{obj:synth_4} (\widehat\tau_{\mathrm{sel}}) and the surrounding prose invoke the constant C_\epsilon before it is defined or quantified anywhere in the setup section, so the selection rule reads as depending on a free symbol. Fix: state at Definition~\ref{obj:synth_4} that C_\epsilon is the fixed-overlap constant produced later in Theorem~\ref{obj:thm:overlap-adaptive-universal-hybrid}/Lemma~\ref{obj:lem:combined-upper-envelope}, and that the selector is only used to state the envelope, not the hybrid estimator itself.

Section-heading register is inconsistent (lowercase 'proofs and auxiliary lemmas', 'verification note', 'Proofs of the main results') and the Definition~\ref{obj:def:hybrid-estimator-handle} block is a single very long unbroken specification mixing calibration constants, cutoffs, pilot sets, and polynomial machinery, which taxes the reader. Fix: normalize heading capitalization and break the hybrid-estimator definition into labelled sub-parts (calibration constants; pilot sets; heavy branch; light branch; truncation).

The abstract says consistency within the stated fixed-interior range is equivalent to d=o(n log n), but the formal theorem requires the sequence to remain eventually inside d <= rho_epsilon n log n; revise the abstract and introduction to say “within the theorem’s range d <= rho_epsilon n log n” each time this equivalence is stated.

The introduction says the lower bound is obtained by transferring a large-alphabet causal lower bound to the unrestricted ATE experiment, but the proof relies on a published one-arm lower bound that is not formalized; make this dependency explicit in the main-text lower-bound paragraph, not only in verification footnotes.

The selected-envelope discussion can read like a near-endpoint minimax statement even though only an upper bound is proved; in the abstract and Theorem 2 discussion, replace language such as “separates” and “upper envelope” with a more explicit sentence: “No matching lower bound is claimed for epsilon_n -> 1/2.”

Definition 1 defines E_{n,d,epsilon} while also quantifying over n,d,epsilon inside the set, which obscures whether these are fixed indices or free parameters; rewrite it as a class for fixed n,d,epsilon: E_{n,d,epsilon} = {P^{\otimes n}: P in Prob(...), P satisfies overlap at epsilon}.

Several proof passages contain Lean-oriented bookkeeping and empty-class conventions that are distracting for an econometrics paper, especially in Lemma 4 and the endpoint proofs; move these details to the verification appendix and keep the statistical proof focused on subclass inclusion, testing, and estimator risk.

The literature positioning is broadly plausible but sometimes generic: the discussion of doubly robust/TMLE and higher-order influence functions should specify that the paper is not comparing to adaptive estimators under structural nuisance assumptions, and should identify exactly which assumptions in those literatures are absent here.

weaknesses: Definitions~\ref{obj:synth_8} and~\ref{obj:synth_4} are literally the same selection rule stated twice, and the paper concedes this in prose ("two equivalent displayed forms of the same deterministic selection rule"). This is an artifact of the extraction pipeline, not a design choice, and a referee will read it as carelessness. Fix: delete Definition~\ref{obj:synth_8}, keep one definition, and update the single cross-reference in Theorem~\ref{obj:thm:overlap-adaptive-universal-hybrid}.

The centered estimator is defined twice with inconsistent placement of the factor 2: Definition~\ref{obj:synth_5} writes $\frac{2}{n}\sum(2A_i-1)(Y_i-1/2)$ while Lemma~\ref{obj:lem:near-randomization-linear-upper} restates it as $\frac{1}{n}\sum 2(2A_i-1)(Y_i-1/2)$. These agree, but a lemma should not silently redefine an object already given a numbered definition. Fix: in the lemma, replace the restated display with "with $\widehat\tau_{\mathrm{ctr}}$ as in Definition~\ref{obj:synth_5}".

Theorem~\ref{obj:thm:sharp-minimax-fixed-interior} quantifies "for every single-observation law $P$" and "every sample law $P^{\otimes n}\in\mathcal E_{n,d,\epsilon}$" even though the conclusion $\mathsf R_{n,d,\epsilon}\le\cdots$ does not mention $P$ at all; the same vacuous binder appears in the parametric-window clause. This makes the statement look like it depends on a law it does not. Fix: drop the $P$ quantifier from the sharp-sandwich and parametric-window clauses, and state the hypotheses purely as conditions on $(n,d,\epsilon)$.

The assumption section asserts overlap, consistency, and conditional exchangeability are "standard" with a single repeated citation to \citet{ZengBalakrishnanHanKennedy2024} in four consecutive paragraphs, and never discusses restrictiveness. In particular, uniform overlap $\epsilon\le\pi_k\le1-\epsilon$ *uniformly over an unboundedly growing alphabet* is a strong and arguably unnatural requirement when many categories carry $O(1/n)$ mass, and this tension is the paper's central phenomenon. Fix: add a paragraph after Assumption~\ref{obj:ass:overlap} discussing what uniform overlap does and does not rule out as $d$ grows, and cite the overlap-critique literature (\citealp{DAmour2021}) there rather than only in the discussion.

The claim in Definition~\ref{obj:def:hybrid-estimator-handle} that "the definition uses no overlap parameter" is repeated three times (setup, Theorem~\ref{obj:thm:sharp-minimax-fixed-interior} discussion, and the paragraph after it) and is stated more strongly than the results support: the estimator is $\epsilon$-free, but its *guarantee* holds only for $n\ge N_\epsilon$ and $d\le\rho_\epsilon n\log n$, both $\epsilon$-dependent, so it is not adaptive over $\epsilon$ in any uniform sense despite the theorem being titled "Universal Hybrid Envelope" and "overlap-adaptive". Fix: state once, near Definition~\ref{obj:def:hybrid-estimator-handle}, that the formula is $\epsilon$-free while the range of validity and constants are not, and rename the theorem to drop "overlap-adaptive".

Definition~\ref{obj:synth_7} contains a typo in the observation alphabet, writing $\{1,\ldots,d\}\times\{0,1\}\times\{0,\ldots,1\}$ in the body while the header uses $\{0,1\}^2$; relatedly, the numerical constants in Definition~\ref{obj:def:hybrid-estimator-handle} ($\lambda_0=256$, $b_0=4096$, $A=6$, $D_A=8\log(27/4)$) are dropped on the reader with no indication of which are essential and which are proof-convenience. Fix: correct the alphabet typo, and add one sentence after the definition saying the constants are not optimized and only their existence matters for the rate.

The introduction promises "the precise verification boundary recorded in the verification appendix", but the verification note ends with the unqualified sentence "All theorem statements and the corresponding formal proofs in this paper are machine-checked in Lean 4", which sits awkwardly against the two footnotes conceding that \citet{ZengBalakrishnanHanKennedy2024}'s lower-bound construction is assumed rather than proved. Fix: replace that final sentence with an explicit statement that the lower-bound scale is an imported hypothesis and that everything downstream of it is conditional on it.

weaknesses: The abstract and introduction advertise the rate for the range `d \le \rho_\epsilon n\log n`, but Lemma~\ref{obj:lem:light-cell-polynomial} and Lemma~\ref{obj:lem:universal-heavy-cell-rate} both fix `c_\epsilon = \rho_\epsilon = 1`, so the theorem's proved range is in fact `d \le n\log n` with an `\epsilon`-free constant. Either state the concrete constant (`\rho_\epsilon=1`) in Theorem~\ref{obj:thm:sharp-minimax-fixed-interior} and note that the binding restriction comes from the imported lower bound's `b_\epsilon`, or explain in a remark why the abstract's `\rho_\epsilon` notation is used for a quantity known to be 1. As written, a reader cannot tell how large the certified regime actually is, and the headline `d = o(n\log n)` consistency threshold is proved only inside a gate of the same order — a fact stated only obliquely as 'within the same displayed range'. Make that circularity explicit in the paragraph following the theorem.

Assumption discussion is thin relative to how much the results depend on it. Assumptions~\ref{obj:ass:consistency} and~\ref{obj:ass:conditional-exchangeability} each receive a single sentence that says only 'this is the standard condition' and cites \citet{ZengBalakrishnanHanKennedy2024} — a statistics paper, not the source of these conditions. Add a short subsection after Assumption~\ref{obj:ass:conditional-exchangeability} discussing (i) what unrestricted discrete confounding means substantively (e.g. many rare covariate cells arising from interacted categorical adjustment sets), (ii) why binary outcomes are not merely a convenience — whether the `d^2/(n^2\log^2 n)` term survives for bounded or unbounded `Y`, and (iii) the fact that Assumption~\ref{obj:ass:overlap} is imposed only on cells with `p_k>0`, so categories of vanishing mass evade the restriction entirely, which is precisely what drives the hard term. Also cite the conditions to their origins (\citet{Rubin1974,RosenbaumRubin1983}) rather than to a recent minimax paper.

The hybrid estimator in Definition~\ref{obj:def:hybrid-estimator-handle} is presented as a wall of calibrated constants (`\lambda_0=256`, `b_0=4096`, `A=6`, `D_A=8\log(27/4)`, `\alpha_0=\min\{1,1/(32\log A),1/(256D_A)\}`) with no explanation of where any of them comes from or which are essential versus artifacts of the proof. Precede the definition with a paragraph giving the three design choices in words — pilot threshold at order `\log n` so mis-classification is polynomially rare; polynomial degree `M \asymp \log n` because that is what balances approximation bias against factorial-moment variance; effective mass scale `B \asymp \log n / n` — and then state that the displayed numerals are one admissible calibration, not tuned recommendations. Without this, the estimator reads as unusable in practice, which undercuts the paper's own emphasis on computability.

Section 3 (Discussion) asserts what fails for competing estimators without any supporting result: 'trimming or stabilization introduces bias whose worst-case size depends on the number of categories' and IPW 'faces the same local denominator problem' are stated flatly, but the paper proves no lower bound for plug-in or IPW estimators. Either downgrade these to explicitly heuristic language ('the analysis suggests', 'we do not prove a lower bound for these estimators') or add a short proposition bounding the plug-in risk from below on a specific sparse-category law. As written these are the strongest-sounding negative claims in the paper and the only unsupported ones.

The paper never reports the sample-size cutoff `N_\epsilon` or its dependence on `\epsilon`, yet every headline claim is conditional on `n \ge N_\epsilon`, and the proof of Lemma~\ref{obj:lem:universal-heavy-cell-rate} sets `N_\epsilon = \max\{8, N_0, N_{\mathrm{bad}}\}` where `N_0` is an implicitly-defined 'least numerical cutoff' from Definition~\ref{obj:def:hybrid-estimator-handle}. Add a remark after Theorem~\ref{obj:thm:sharp-minimax-fixed-interior} stating whether `N_\epsilon` is explicit and computable, and how badly `C_\epsilon` degrades as `\epsilon \downarrow 0` — the proof's `K_\epsilon = 16/\epsilon + 12 + \epsilon^{-4}` implies an `\epsilon^{-4}` blow-up that is invisible in the prose and is directly relevant to the paper's own claim about limited-overlap concerns.

Definition~\ref{obj:synth_7} contains a typo — the alphabet is written `\{1,\ldots,d\}\times\{0,1\}\times\{0,\ldots,1\}`, mixing `\{0,1\}` and `\{0,\ldots,1\}` in one line — and the appendix section headings are lowercase ('proofs and auxiliary lemmas', 'verification note') while all others are capitalized. Separately, `\widehat\tau_{\mathrm{ctr}}` is defined in Definition~\ref{obj:synth_5} as `(2/n)\sum(2A_i-1)(Y_i-1/2)` but in Lemma~\ref{obj:lem:near-randomization-linear-upper} as `(1/n)\sum 2(2A_i-1)(Y_i-1/2)`; these agree, but the gratuitous restatement invites the reader to check. Fix the typo, capitalize the headings, and state the centered estimator once by reference.

Introduction/Main results: several prose claims say the estimator is “overlap-adaptive” or “uses no overlap parameter,” but the selected estimator in Definition synth_4 depends on fixed-interior constants C_epsilon and epsilon; clarify that only the hybrid formula is epsilon-free, while the selected near-randomization rule is oracle/calibrated to epsilon and theorem constants.

Abstract/Introduction: the phrase “unrestricted marginal distribution” is accurate for X, but the model also restricts treatment/outcome to binary and imposes uniform categorywise overlap; revise headline prose to consistently say “unrestricted covariate marginal subject to binary observed data and overlap,” especially in the abstract and first two introduction paragraphs.

Theorem sharp-minimax-fixed-interior: the sharp sandwich bullet begins “For every n,d, every single-observation law P...” although P is unused in the displayed risk statement except through the class; remove the extraneous P or rewrite to avoid suggesting a pointwise-in-P result rather than a minimax class result.

Setup definitions: there are several syntactic/register issues from generated Lean-facing prose, e.g. Definition synth_7 uses \{0,\ldots,1\} for Y, Section A title is lowercase, and many labels like synth_1/synth_7 are exposed; clean these to conventional paper notation and human-readable labels before circulation.

Positioning: the literature discussion cites broad semiparametric and large-alphabet work but does not precisely state what is new relative to ZengBalakrishnanHanKennedy2024, which supplies the lower-bound construction; add one paragraph distinguishing the paper’s upper-bound/estimator/formalization contribution from the imported lower-bound ingredient.

\] The upper bound is attained by a computable split-sample hybrid estimator: empirical ratios are used on pilot-certified heavy categories, while light categories are estimated by a Chebyshev-polynomial continuation implemented through factorial moments. → \] Under the same fixed-interior calibration conditions, the upper bound is attained by a computable split-sample hybrid estimator: empirical ratios are used on pilot-certified heavy categories, while light categories are estimated by a Chebyshev-polynomial continuation implemented through factorial moments.

weaknesses: Abstract and Theorem 1 both assert the rate holds 'for d ≤ ρ_ε n log n', but the proof of Lemma~\ref{obj:lem:universal-heavy-cell-rate} and Lemma~\ref{obj:lem:light-cell-polynomial} set ρ_ε = 1 and c_ε = 1 respectively, so the actual admissible range is a fixed absolute constant, not an ε-dependent one, and the reader cannot tell from the theorem statement how large the window really is. Fix: state in the theorem or a remark that ρ_ε may be taken to be 1 (and c_ε = 1), and explain why the ε-indexed notation is retained.

The paper never explains why the growth ceiling d ≤ ρ_ε n log n is needed, nor what is known or conjectured above it — yet the consistency-threshold claim 'consistent estimation is possible exactly when d = o(n log n)' is stated in the Introduction as if it characterized the whole boundary, when in fact the ceiling coincides with the threshold so the 'only if' direction is never tested outside the certified window. Fix: add a paragraph in Section 3 after Theorem 1 stating explicitly that d ≥ ρ_ε n log n is outside the certified range, that the equivalence is vacuous on one side, and that inconsistency for d ≫ n log n is not established here.

Assumption discussion is thin and boilerplate: each of Assumptions~\ref{obj:ass:iid-sampling}--\ref{obj:ass:conditional-exchangeability} gets one sentence of the form 'this is the standard X condition' followed by the same citation, with no discussion of when uniform overlap at a fixed ε is plausible for a growing alphabet — note that requiring ε ≤ π_k ≤ 1-ε for all d categories simultaneously becomes a strong joint restriction as d grows with n, and that overlap in population does not prevent empirical non-overlap (a point the Discussion makes but the Setup does not). Fix: expand Section 2 with a paragraph on the restrictiveness of uniform overlap under growing d, and forward-reference the empirical-overlap discussion.

Definition~\ref{obj:def:hybrid-estimator-handle} dumps a dozen unexplained numerical constants (A=6, λ₀=256, b₀=4096, D_A=8log(27/4), α₀) with no indication of where they come from or which are essential versus artifacts of the proof; the appendix then introduces further magic numbers (K_c involving 8192², K=450). Fix: add a sentence after the definition classifying the constants — which set the pilot threshold, which set the polynomial degree, which are slack — and state plainly that none are optimized.

Definition~\ref{obj:synth_7} contains a typographical error: the alphabet is written both as {1,…,d}×{0,1}² in the header and as {1,…,d}×{0,1}×{0,…,1} in the body, where '{0,…,1}' is presumably '{0,1}'. Fix: unify to {1,…,d}×{0,1}×{0,1} throughout.

The near-randomization envelope is repeated nearly verbatim four times (abstract, introduction, Theorem~\ref{obj:thm:overlap-adaptive-universal-hybrid}, Discussion), each time with the same 'this is only an upper bound, the transition is not characterized' caveat; this is honest but consumes disproportionate space for a secondary result, while the substantively more interesting question — what the hybrid estimator's finite-sample behavior looks like, or any numerical illustration of the d²/(n²log²n) term's magnitude — receives none. Fix: compress the endpoint discussion to one statement in Section 3 plus one caveat sentence in Section 4, and use the freed space for a short numerical table showing the two risk terms at representative (n,d).

Positioning cites Robins–Ritov (1997) and the higher-order influence function literature as 'especially relevant' and 'complementary' but never states concretely what those results would give in this exact finite-alphabet model, leaving the comparison rhetorical. Fix: in Section 4, add one or two sentences stating what rate, if any, a HOIF or DML estimator is known to achieve for this estimand under Assumptions~\ref{obj:ass:iid-sampling}--\ref{obj:ass:conditional-exchangeability} alone, or say explicitly that no such comparison rate is available.

Introduction, first main-result paragraph: the statement says the theorem gives the sharp rate over the finite-alphabet overlap class, but only later says this is within d <= rho_epsilon n log n and n >= N_epsilon. Move those conditions into the first sentence of the claim to avoid an unconditional impression.

Abstract and introduction: the phrase 'consistency threshold d=o(n log n)' is only proved within the eventual calibration range d <= rho_epsilon n log n and fixed interior epsilon. State it as 'within this fixed-interior calibration range' every time, or readers may infer a global impossibility/possibility statement beyond the theorem's support.

Literature positioning: the connection to ZengBalakrishnanHanKennedy2024 is described as a transfer from a fixed-sample construction, but the paper relies substantively on that external lower bound. Add a short paragraph explaining exactly what is imported, what is new in this paper, and why the transfer is nontrivial but not a new lower-bound construction.

Setup/assumptions: the causal assumptions are stated, but the minimax class itself is an observed-data class; clarify more explicitly that the causal overlay is only interpretive and that the formal risk is over observed laws satisfying overlap, not over full-data laws satisfying exchangeability and consistency.

Estimator definition section: Definition of the hybrid estimator is too long and implementation-heavy for the main text. Move some numerical constants and polynomial-continuation details to an appendix, leaving the main text with the estimator logic and a precise reference to the full calibrated definition.

Writing/register: many Lean artifacts and labels such as synth_7, obj:, leanref, and theorem-local verification comments interrupt the flow of a serious working paper. Consider hiding these in a verification appendix or using conventional theorem labels in the main paper while preserving machine-verification traceability elsewhere.

problems.detail: The lower-bound scale is imported from the fixed-sample construction of \citet{ZengBalakrishnanHanKennedy2024}; the new contribution is the matching computable upper bound and the resulting sharp fixed-interior regimes. → The lower-bound scale is imported from the fixed-sample construction of \citet{ZengBalakrishnanHanKennedy2024}; the paper proves a matching computable upper bound and the resulting fixed-interior regimes.

The main result is that, for each fixed \(0<\epsilon<1/2\), the sharp minimax rate is not the plug-in scale suggested by summing \(d\) ordinary cellwise errors. → The main result is that, for each fixed \(0<\epsilon<1/2\), the sharp minimax rate is \(n^{-1}+d^2/(n^2\log^2 n)\) under the stated fixed-interior calibration range.

The logarithmic improvement comes from the same broad source as in large-alphabet nonsmooth functional estimation: polynomial approximation can outperform direct substitution when many cells are weakly sampled \citep{Paninski2003,JiaoVenkatHanWeissman2015,WuYang2016,HanJiaoWeissman2020}. → The estimator uses polynomial approximation on weakly sampled cells, a device related to large-alphabet nonsmooth functional estimation \citep{Paninski2003,JiaoVenkatHanWeissman2015,WuYang2016,HanJiaoWeissman2020}.

Here the nonsmooth object is not entropy or support size, but the four-cell ATE contribution \[ p_k(\mu_{1k}-\mu_{0k}). → Here the cell contribution is the four-cell ATE term \[ p_k(\mu_{1k}-\mu_{0k}).

This paper closes the corresponding upper-bound gap by constructing the ratio-polynomial hybrid estimator and deriving the sharp fixed-interior consistency and parametric-rate regimes. → This paper constructs the ratio-polynomial hybrid estimator and derives the fixed-interior consistency and parametric-rate regimes.

Once the number of categories is large enough, the difficulty is no longer only the variance of an efficient influence-function average; it is also the problem of estimating many small, poorly sampled conditional contrasts. → Outside the parametric window, the proved minimax rate includes the additional term \(d^2/(n^2(\log n)^2)\), reflecting the cost of many small categories in this benchmark.

This distinction clarifies what fails for direct plug-in and weighting estimators in the unrestricted discrete model. → This distinction motivates comparing the hybrid construction with direct plug-in and weighting estimators in the unrestricted discrete model.

The minimax statement isolates that difficulty in a finite-alphabet benchmark: even with exact overlap and binary outcomes, unrestricted covariate heterogeneity creates a nonregular term \(d^2/(n^2(\log n)^2)\). → The minimax statement isolates that difficulty in a finite-alphabet benchmark: for each fixed \(0<\epsilon<1/2\) under the calibrated range, binary outcomes and unrestricted covariate heterogeneity yield the term \(d^2/(n^2(\log n)^2)\).

The overlap constant in Theorem~\ref{obj:thm:sharp-minimax-fixed-interior} is fixed away from \(1/2\), so the theorem is not driven by near-nonidentification from propensities close to zero or one. → The overlap constant in Theorem~\ref{obj:thm:sharp-minimax-fixed-interior} is fixed with \(0<\epsilon<1/2\); for each such fixed \(\epsilon\), the theorem is not an asymptotic statement about propensities approaching zero or one.

weaknesses: The abstract and intro call the rate 'sharp' and 'minimax' while the entire lower-bound scale is imported unverified from Zeng et al. (2024) — the paper's own contribution is the upper bound plus a transfer argument. The title itself ('Sharp Minimax Rates') overstates ownership. Fix: retitle to foreground the constructive/attainability contribution (e.g. 'A Computable Estimator Attaining the Minimax Rate for ATE under Unrestricted Discrete Confounding') and, in the abstract's first rate display, attach a parenthetical noting the lower bound is cited, not proved here.

The calibration range d <= rho_eps n log n is stated everywhere but never interpreted, and the proofs of Lemma~\ref{obj:lem:light-cell-polynomial} and Lemma~\ref{obj:lem:universal-heavy-cell-rate} both set c_eps = rho_eps = 1, so the operative constraint is d <= n log n while Theorem~\ref{obj:thm:sharp-minimax-fixed-interior} presents rho_eps as an unspecified eps-dependent constant. Fix: add a remark after Theorem 1 stating the achieved value rho_eps = 1 (bounded below by the lower-bound gate b_eps), and explain in words what regime d > n log n corresponds to and why it is excluded.

Assumption discussion is boilerplate: each of Assumptions~\ref{obj:ass:iid-sampling}--\ref{obj:ass:conditional-exchangeability} is followed by a one-sentence 'this is the standard X condition' with the same citation, and none discusses restrictiveness. Most consequentially, the binary-outcome restriction Y in {0,1} is never justified or discussed, yet it is what produces the specific d^2/(n^2 log^2 n) exponent; likewise the finite-alphabet-with-no-structure premise is asserted as a benchmark without argument that it is empirically relevant. Fix: add a short subsection after Definition~\ref{obj:def:experiment-class} discussing (i) why binary outcomes, and what is conjectured for bounded outcomes, (ii) an empirical example where d grows with n and the covariate is genuinely unstructured, (iii) the fact that uniform overlap over categories with p_k -> 0 is a population condition with no finite-sample content.

The hybrid estimator in Definition~\ref{obj:def:hybrid-estimator-handle} arrives as a wall of hardcoded numerical constants (lambda_0 = 256, b_0 = 4096, D_A = 8 log(27/4), A = 6) with no explanation of what any of them controls or how they were chosen; a reader cannot tell which are essential and which are proof-convenience slack. Fix: precede the definition with a paragraph giving the three design ideas (pilot threshold at order log n, degree M ~ alpha_0 log n, mass scale B ~ log n / m_1) and relegate the exact numerals to a display with a footnote that they are chosen for proof convenience, not optimized.

Positioning omits any discussion of practical performance: the paper claims computability with an O(d M(n)^4) operation count but never notes that M(n)^4 with M ~ alpha_0 log(en) and alpha_0 <= 1/(256 D_A) means M = 2 for essentially all realistic n, so the light branch degenerates to a degree-2 polynomial in practice. Fix: add a remark in Section 'Discussion and extensions' computing M(n) at, say, n = 10^3, 10^6, 10^12, and stating honestly that the log-factor gain is asymptotic and the estimator is not offered as a practical recommendation; alternatively report a small numerical illustration.

Section 'verification note' asserts broad coverage in prose but names no Lean declarations, no repository, and no commit, while simultaneously saying declaration names 'are not part of the statistical argument' — this makes the machine-verification claim unfalsifiable for a referee. Fix: add a crosswalk table mapping each numbered Definition/Lemma/Theorem to its Lean declaration name and file, plus a URL and commit hash, and state explicitly whether any 'sorry' or axiom beyond the cited Zeng et al. input appears in the development.

The near-randomization envelope is heavily hedged (five separate disclaimers across abstract, intro, Theorem~\ref{obj:thm:overlap-adaptive-universal-hybrid}, Lemma~\ref{obj:lem:combined-upper-envelope}, and the discussion) yet still occupies a prominent place; given that it is a two-line max of two already-stated bounds under an oracle selector, the repetition reads defensively and inflates its apparent importance. Fix: cut it from the abstract entirely, state it once as a short corollary to Theorem 2, and keep a single sentence in the discussion noting the open triangular-array question.

weaknesses: The abstract and Introduction both assert the sharp rate holds 'for each fixed 0<\epsilon<1/2' with constants \rho_\epsilon, but Lemma~\ref{obj:lem:light-cell-polynomial} sets c_\epsilon=1 and Lemma~\ref{obj:lem:universal-heavy-cell-rate} sets \rho_\epsilon=1, so the actual proved growth gate is the \epsilon-free d\le n\log n. Fix: either state the gate as d\le n\log n throughout (which is a stronger, cleaner result) or explain in a remark why the \epsilon-indexed notation is retained despite the proofs delivering a universal constant. As written a referee will suspect the constants were not tracked.

The overlap condition (Assumption~\ref{obj:ass:overlap}) is asserted to be 'standard' with a single citation and no discussion of what it rules out, yet the entire paper's contrast between the interior and the \epsilon=1/2 endpoint turns on it. Fix: add a paragraph after Assumption~\ref{obj:ass:overlap} discussing (i) that it is a population, not empirical, condition — the point later made only in Section 4's plug-in discussion — (ii) that it binds only on categories with p_k>0, so it places no restriction on how much mass sits in near-empty categories, and (iii) an example of an observational design where uniform \epsilon across a growing alphabet is or is not plausible.

Assumptions~\ref{obj:ass:consistency} and~\ref{obj:ass:conditional-exchangeability} each receive one sentence saying they are 'standard' with the same citation, and their restrictiveness is never discussed. Since Proposition~\ref{obj:prop:two-category-confounding} shows the causal and observational objects diverge, the paper needs to say what happens when conditional exchangeability fails — the minimax result is untouched (it is a statement about the observational functional \tau(P)) but the causal reading is lost. Fix: state explicitly in Section 2 that Assumptions 3-4 are used only for interpretation and that all minimax content is assumption-free of them.

Definition~\ref{obj:def:hybrid-estimator-handle} spans a full page of numerical constants (\lambda_0=256, b_0=4096, D_A=8\log(27/4), A=6) with no explanation of where any of them comes from or which are essential versus artifacts of the proof. Fix: move the constant specification to the appendix, keep in the main text only the structural description (pilot threshold of order \log n, degree M(n)\asymp\log n, factorial-moment plug-in), and add one sentence per constant group indicating its role, so a reader can see the estimator's shape without parsing the calibration.

Definition~\ref{obj:def:finite-alphabet-probability-laws} writes the alphabet as '\{1,\ldots,d\}\times\{0,1\}\times\{0,\ldots,1\}' — the last factor is a typo for \{0,1\}; and Definition~\ref{obj:def:centered-estimator} writes \widehat\tau_{\mathrm{ctr}}=(2/n)\sum(2A_i-1)(Y_i-1/2) while Lemma~\ref{obj:lem:near-randomization-linear-upper} restates it as (1/n)\sum 2(2A_i-1)(Y_i-1/2). These agree but the reader must check; fix both, and use one form consistently.

Section~4 (Discussion) argues the hybrid beats plug-in and IPW in this class but offers no quantitative statement — no rate, no lower bound, no simulation — for those competitors, so 'trimming or stabilization introduces bias whose worst-case size depends on the number of categories' is an unsupported assertion in a paper whose selling point is proved sharpness. Fix: either cite the specific result in \citet{ZengBalakrishnanHanKennedy2024} that quantifies the plug-in/IPW/DR rates in this exact class (they analyze these estimators, per the Introduction) with the rate displayed, or soften to an explicitly heuristic remark.
